title: PART TWO: THE NETWORK
content: Three weeks after Sterling's arrest, Chloe was back in Brooklyn.

The apartment was smaller than she remembered. The ceiling still had the water stain from the upstairs neighbor who'd overflowed their bathtub in 2023. The Wi-Fi password was still the same — *eattherich2020* — and the plant in the window, a real one this time, was dead. She'd been gone fourteen days and the plant had given up on day six.
Chloe understood the feeling.

The olanzapine had fully cleared her system, but the memory of it hadn't. Some nights she woke up at 2 AM with the phantom taste of the yellow pill on her tongue and had to sit in the dark for ten minutes, reminding herself where she was. Not Room 214. Not the lavender-scented air. Not the camera with its red heartbeat.

She'd kept the fake plant. The one from her room. Holloway had cleared out patient belongings after the evacuation, but Vasquez had grabbed it before the county sealed the building. The pills were still inside — six of them, white and pale blue and yellow, tucked into the hollow spine. Chloe kept the plant on her desk like a war trophy.
Her phone, which had been locked in Holloway's drawer for two weeks, had seventeen thousand notifications.

The TikTok had hit twelve million views while she was locked up. #FreeChloe had trended for three days. Anna Delgado's article had been shared four hundred thousand times. Someone had made a Chloe fancam — a *fancam* — set to a Charli XCX song, intercutting clips of her original video with news footage of Sterling's arrest. Chloe watched it four times before she admitted to herself that she was crying.
Not because of the fancam. Because of the comments.
*"My parents sent me to a place like this in Arizona. Two years. I'm still not okay."*
*"I thought I was the only one. I thought I was crazy. I've been carrying this for twelve years."*
*"Where do we find help for this? Not psychiatric help. The other kind. The 'what happened to me was a crime' kind."*
Thousands of comments. Thousands of women. Every one of them saying the same thing: *I thought I was alone.*
Chloe closed the app and called Layla.

The six of them met in a diner in Williamsburg three days later — the kind with vinyl booths and bottomless coffee and a jukebox that only played Fleetwood Mac. Chloe had picked it because the ceiling didn't have cameras.

Layla arrived first, tablet in hand, fingers already tapping. She'd been out for three weeks and in that time had built a secure messaging app for the group, a private server for their communications, and a database of every private psychiatric facility in the United States that Rachel had identified as "Serenity-adjacent" — high-end, family-referral-based, exclusively female patient populations under thirty.

"Eleven facilities," Layla said, sliding the tablet across the table. "California, Connecticut, Colorado, Arizona, Florida, New York, Oregon, Washington, Illinois, Texas, and one in Switzerland that I'm pretty sure is the same model but with better marketing."

"Eleven," Sophia said, arriving in a blazer that cost more than the diner's entire furniture budget. She'd been released to her own apartment — not her family's, which she'd disowned via a press release that Anna Delgado had published on The Verge — and was in the process of suing her father for fraudulent imprisonment. The lawsuit was being handled by Maria Okonkwo's office. Sophia had gone from porcelain doll to plaintiff in three weeks, and she was thriving.

"Eleven is a lot," Maya said, sliding into the booth next to Sophia. She'd been staying with her aunt Maria, who was using Maya's testimony as the foundation for the Sterling prosecution. "We just got out of one. We're not ready for eleven."

"We're not going after eleven at once," Rachel said. She'd arrived last, because she'd stopped to pick up a package — a new copy of The New England Journal of Medicine, which she'd bought to replace the one she'd been reading for three years. "We're going after one. The one that's closest to falling."
"And which one is that?"

"Aurora Behavioral Health," Rachel said. "Colorado. I've been tracking them for two years. They're Serenity's sister facility — same model, same referral network, same families. Sterling and Aurora's founder were colleagues at McKinsey. They built their facilities together. Same playbook. Different states."
"And Aurora is 'closest to falling' because?"

"Because their founder just died." Rachel opened her new journal — empty pages, ready to be filled. "Heart attack. Three weeks ago. The facility is in transition. New management. Uncertain leadership. It's the moment when institutions are most vulnerable."
Vasquez, who'd been quiet through most of the conversation, put her coffee down. "Before we plan the next operation, there's something you need to understand."
"What?"
"Sterling's arrest made the news. So did your faces. All six of you. Chloe's TikTok has twelve million views. The article has four hundred thousand shares. You're not anonymous anymore."
"We were never anonymous," Chloe said. "That was the point."

"I know. But the families — the ones who sent their daughters to these facilities — they're not sitting still. I've been monitoring private security channels. There's talk. Law firms. PR firms. 'Reputation management' specialists. The families are organizing."
"Organizing how?"

"They're forming a coalition. A legal defense fund. They're calling it the 'Family Wellness Alliance.'" Vasquez's mouth twisted on the name like it tasted bad. "Their argument is that you six are anti-psychiatry extremists who are harassing families for seeking legitimate medical treatment for their children."
"Their children who are adults."
"Who cares about facts when you have PR firms?" Vasquez leaned back. "Sterling was one battle. The families are the war. And the war isn't in the facilities. It's in the court of public opinion."

Chloe was quiet for a moment. The diner hummed around them — the jukebox switching to "Landslide," the coffee machine hissing, the cook in the back yelling something in Spanish about an order of hash browns. Normal sounds. The sounds of a world that had kept turning while she was locked up.
"Then we fight there too," she said. "On their turf. Public opinion is the one thing I actually know how to do."
"You mean TikTok?" Maya asked.

"I mean everything. TikTok. Twitter. The Verge. Last Week Tonight. Every platform where people listen. Every outlet that ran Anna's story. Every woman who commented on my video saying she thought she was alone." Chloe leaned forward. "They're forming a coalition? Fine. So are we. But ours will be bigger. Because there are more of us than there are of them. There always have been. They just made sure we never found each other."

She looked around the table — Layla with her database of eleven facilities, Sophia with her press release and her lawsuit, Maya with her aunt the ADA, Rachel with three years of memorized patient files, Vasquez with her security channels and her inside knowledge of how the enemy operated.

"We're not six anymore," Chloe said. "We're the beginning of something. And the next time a wealthy family tries to make their daughter disappear, they're going to find out that disappearing someone is a lot harder when everyone is watching."
Layla's fingers stopped tapping. "So what do we call ourselves?"
Chloe looked at her. Then at the tablet, with its map of eleven red dots — eleven facilities, eleven Serenities, eleven places where women were being erased.
"We already have a name," she said. "Anna gave it to us in the article headline."
She turned the tablet around. The cached headline was still there, from the article that had launched everything:
**THE WOMEN OF SERENITY**
"We're the Women of Serenity," Chloe said. "And we're just getting started."
